author: @Teknopottu
text:
Great video but cannot say the same thing about audio. 

Volume had to be way up because your voice was faded and wind gusts caused terrible popping on the speakers. Hurt my ears, hopefully not my speakers. 

What kind of microphone muff do you use? Hairy one is better in windy conditions, in my best knowledge. Also, you could remove the audio peaks in your software, some do it automatic if allowed.

author: @pgprentice
text: Get some 3M UV stabilized clear plastic wrap and put that over the lens.  This will stop the plastic from being damaged by UV and the wrap can be replaced when needed.

text: Really useful vid Luke thanks!

author: @campbellpaul
text: I think maybe the trick to protecting most plastics from the elements is silicone gel.  It acts as a conditioner by repelling moisture and air and is not made from petroleum which has a tendency to quickly degrade many types of polycarbonates, as petrol derivatives tend to quickly emulsify into one another.  A two-step process of first eliminating the outer-layer with a mild abrasive seems like the way to go, and being sure to remove any excess silica to prevent dirt adhesion.  I will try T-Cut just the same, as it does sound like a great product.  Cheers and thanks for the video.

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: @ianbutler1983 Interesting.  That means you just need to wash it well afterwards and coat with a UV varnish.  Thanks for pointing that out!!!

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey Yes those repair kits are not only expensive, they take a long time to complete with lots of sanding.  It would be nice to spray a little bug spray and wipe them clean.  Only thing I would be careful about is if some lights have a material that might be adversely affected by the spray, and perhaps also make it crystal clear to wash it really well with soap afterwards, and to rinse really really well before coating with UV protect.  Perhaps you can do a test of different UV protect prays.  It's really hard to get anything to stick to plastic.  So testing UV spray s would be helpful.  The varnish sounds promising.  There is also a plastic primer which is usually used before painting plastic so need a clear primer as well.  Even better, a clear primer you can mix with UV sauce!  😂

author: @saiello2061
text: I always use Autosol metal polish on clear plastic with great results, but any brand will do really. I've used it on watch faces, motorcycle clocks and other transparent items. Work in small circles, and slowly, to reduce heat build up so you can avoid creating that "orange peel" surface effect. If scratches are too deep for the polish alone, I use some 1200 grit "wet and dry" abrasive paper. Gently rub down over a wider area than the scratch for blending in so you don't create a hollow. Use water containing a tiny tiny amount of washing up liquid, flushing the part often. When you have a nice, soft, "opaque" velvety surface finish when dry, you can finish off with the polish. I don't think I've come across a better combo 👍😁.

author: @williamarmstrong7199
text: How long did it take?

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: This took me, more or less an hour per headlight. Although this was done by hand, I can imagine if you have a automatic buffer this process would take much less time! :)

author: @noncog1
text: Be sure to replace the protective coating thats worn away by spraying them with a uv resistant automotive clear coat, otherwise it wont last too long

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: was going to say I did the same after using various levels of sand paper and water down to 3000 grit to get a smooth surface, though I used just a generic UV protection clear spray paint, and it is still holding up after over 6 years LOL

replies.text: if they are the same material then its a clear polycarbonate plastic housing over the lamps. What I want really now is to finish my vapor deposition chamber so I can make mirror finishes on reflectors LOL (go fully LED with my own breed of LEDs that are much brighter but so they are still focused where they are supposed to be, unlike most headlights that blind on coming traffic.

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Polycarbonate plastic is degraded by UV which causes it to craze. Therefore, it is important to spay on a fresh coat of UV block varnish which the T-cut removed.

replies.author: @noncog1
replies.text: Exactly, an automotive uv resistant clearcoat works

author: @matthewellisor5835
text:
Try applying "303 Aerospace" to the plastic. I've had great results using it.
It seems a bit pricey at around 20-25USD (Guessing around 16-20 pounds? I didn't trouble to look up the conversion.) but one bottle will last a very long time and I'd rather spray and wipe instead of getting out the polishing stuff.

author: @richardlingham9400
text: Done this myself with a mechanical buffer, but I took the headlights out first. They will be great for about 3 months of winter before they need redoing but then it’s only needs a few minutes to get them back

replies.author: @noncog1
replies.text: Spray them with a uv resistant automotive clear coat after and it will last much longer
